title: Randomized Algorithms, Approximation, and Honest Guarantees · Complexity Checkpoint
summary: Choose scalable methods when exact optimization is too expensive or adversarial. Every item uses a fresh case and misconception-specific distractors. Planned artifact: Record independent decisions and explanations for a probabilistic and approximation guarantee report. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Distinguish Las Vegas and Monte Carlo guarantees, failure probability, and expected cost.
Analyze random pivot selection against input-order adversaries and seeded tests.
Reduce independent error probability through repeated trials and justified aggregation.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

starterFiles.python:

steps.title: Reconstruct the defensible process
steps.instruction: Order the Randomized Models correctness-and-cost proof for this quiz task before implementing it.
steps.why: Correct order keeps Randomized Models assumptions visible before execution and verifies its result against a boundary case afterward.

content.text: Run 30 seeded trials on an adversarial ordering. Report expected cost, observed distribution, error probability, and approximation guarantee as separate claims.

content.title: Ordering constraint
content.text: Order work so Randomized Models has a declared contract before execution and a boundary or counterexample after it.

hints: Name the Randomized Models precondition or invariant that the quiz case must preserve.

Trace this concrete case before choosing: Run 30 seeded trials on an adversarial ordering. Report expected cost, observed distribution, error probability, and approximation guarantee as separate claims.
Use the documented misconception as a counterexample target: Randomized algorithms are merely nondeterministic heuristics.

options.text: Define input, output, preconditions, size, and the operation being counted
Choose a correct baseline and state its loop or representation invariant
Trace the candidate on a minimal instance and derive time and space cost
Attack it with boundary and adversarial cases, then justify any optimization
